text:
KINGS NEUROSCIENCES CENTRE


Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Priya Mehta
Riverside Health Centre

12 Riverside Way, Southwark, London SE1 5AB
Dear Dr Priya Mehta
Mr Alan Brewer, DOB: 17-03-1956, Hospital No: H392771 NHS No. 7482195361
Flat 3, 28 Willow Court, London SE1 3DZ 

I reviewed the above patient in the Neurology Clinic today. He is a newly retired teacher who lives alone and reports a very regular daily routine with reduced social contact since retirement. He keeps a diary of symptoms, sleep, and medications, and uses a pill organiser. Since our last review in April, he has not described any further events suggestive of seizures. He specifically reports no collapses, no witnessed convulsions, no episodes of impaired awareness, and no unexplained injuries or tongue biting. He has maintained his usual activity levels, walks daily, and notes his sleep is better structured.

Medication adherence is reported as good with no missed doses. He brings pharmacy records that align with this. He remains on Levetiracetam 1000 mg b.d. and Lamotrigine 150 mg b.d. He denies depressive symptoms but mentions occasional low mood and loneliness related to reduced social interaction; he attributes this to the transition to retirement rather than medication. He denies new neurological symptoms. No rash, no diplopia, no ataxia, and no gastrointestinal upset. Mild morning tiredness is reported but is improving with earlier bedtime.

Examination today was unremarkable. Vitals stable. Neurological examination normal. Weight stable compared with the previous visit. He has continued to abstain from driving and is aware of DVLA guidance; he will notify us before considering any change.

Given the stability of his symptoms and absence of events since the prior assessment, I have made no changes to his current antiepileptic regimen. We discussed strategies to maintain regular sleep and hydration, and he will continue his diary. He is open to contact with our epilepsy specialist nurse for check-ins, particularly to address isolation and practical self-management. Routine bloods (FBC, U&Es, LFTs) will be arranged via his GP in the next fortnight to monitor baseline parameters while on current therapy.

I will review him again in six months, or sooner if he experiences any episodes suggestive of seizure activity (e.g. unresponsive spells, unexplained injuries, witnessed convulsions, or sudden confusion). He understands to seek urgent care for any prolonged event.

Yours sincerely

entities.text: Since our last review in April, he has not described any further events suggestive of seizures.

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter explicitly states there have been no further events suggestive of seizures since the last review. Clinic date is 02 Oct 2025; the last review was in April (year implied 2025). The seizure-free interval is approximately 5 to 6 months depending on the exact April date, which is not specified. Because the 6-month threshold cannot be confirmed precisely, this meets 'seizure free for multiple month' per the instruction to use 'multiple' when the exact duration is unclear but spans months.
seizure_frequency_number: Since our last review in April, he has not described any further events suggestive of seizures.
reference: No seizures reported since last evaluation
